flags: No cut list by design — every dimension comes from the builder's own drawer and jars, so no numbers could be added beyond the 3/4" x 3/4" strip section named in the materials.

Quantity worth confirming: the materials list 4 board feet of 3/4" x 3/4" strip. A board foot is 144 cubic inches, so at a 3/4" x 3/4" section one board foot is about 21 linear feet of this strip and 4 board feet is roughly 85 linear feet — a very large quantity for one drawer insert.

Nothing in the plan states how the jars sit (upright, tilted, or lying across the strips); the rewrite describes the tilt the plan's own description calls for without asserting a jar orientation beyond 'label readable'.

ops.title: Measure the drawer
ops.body: Take three measurements off the drawer itself, because this project has no cut list at all and every piece is sized from these numbers. Measure the clear inside width side to side, the clear inside depth front to back, and the vertical clearance — how tall something can stand in the drawer and still let it close under whatever is above it. The last one is the measurement people skip and then discover too late: the strips are only 3/4" x 3/4", so the frame adds 3/4" under the jar, and the jar plus its tilt has to clear the cabinet rail above. Write all three down before you pick up the saw.

ops.title: Repeat to fill the drawer
ops.body: Measure the front-to-back size of the finished frame — it is the jar-tilt gap you settled on plus the 3/4" thickness of each of the two long strips — then divide the drawer's inside depth by that figure to work out how many frames will fit. Build that many, cutting every long strip to the same drawer width and every connecting piece to the same gap so the frames are interchangeable and sit in any order. Cut and glue in batches rather than one frame at a time; the clamping and drying is the slow part here, not the sawing.

ops.title: Add spacers and load the drawer
ops.body: Set all the finished frames into the drawer side by side and measure whatever gap is left between the last frame and the drawer side. Cut loose spacer strips to that length and drop them in — they get no glue, just a snug friction fit so nothing shifts when the drawer is opened and closed. Sand the top edges and any sharp corners with the 120 grit so jars slide rather than catch, then load the jars in and check the drawer still closes fully with the tallest jar in place.

baseStepTitles: Add spacers and load the drawer

flags: No cut list by design — the description says every dimension derives from the chosen basin, so the rewrite gives the derivation rather than numbers.
No wood glue appears in the materials, though a pocket-hole cabinet normally takes glue at every joint. Candidate to add.
The original knob step calls for 'an end cap' that is not in the materials; the only line is 'Machine screw and nuts'.

No tool for cutting the interior basin opening is listed — the essential tools are a circular saw, pocket-hole jig, and drill only. A jigsaw or hole saw is effectively required and is not on the list.
No fastener is listed for mounting the pipe flange to the cabinet top, and none for securing the pump or tubing.
No drain is described; the basin holds the water and the pump recirculates it, which is what the 3 ft of tubing supports.

ops.title: Choose the basin and size the cabinet
ops.body: Pick the plastic container first — everything else in this build is measured off it, which is why there is no cut list. Set it upside down on your sheet stock and trace the rim; the cabinet top has to be at least that footprint plus enough margin behind the basin for the faucet flange to sit flat, and enough on each side that the top does not look skimpy. Set the cabinet height from the child who will use it rather than from the basin, so the rim lands at about their waist and they can reach into it without stretching. Write down the top's length and width and the cabinet height before cutting anything, because the front, back and sides all come off those three numbers.

ops.title: Cut the cabinet pieces
ops.body: Cut the top to the length and width you recorded, then the front and back to the top's length by the cabinet height, and the two sides to that same height by the top's depth. Decide now whether the front and back overlap the side edges or sit between them, and subtract two board thicknesses from whichever pair ends up captured — get this backwards and the finished cabinet is two board thicknesses off in one direction. Then mark the basin opening in the top: trace the container's rim, then draw a second line inside that one, set in by the width of the container's lip, so the basin drops through and hangs by its rim. Cut to the inner line and test-fit the basin before you assemble anything, because an opening cut slightly small can be filed out and one cut large cannot be put back.

ops.title: Build the cabinet
ops.body: Set the pocket-hole jig for the actual thickness of your stock and drill the ends of the front, back and sides, keeping every hole on an inside face where it will not show. Assemble in the order the plan gives — the top first, then the front and back to the top, then the sides — so the top stays the reference face and the basin opening ends up where you cut it. Check the box for square by measuring both diagonals across the top before the last screws go in; a racked cabinet will not stand flat and the basin will not seat level in its opening.

ops.title: Build the faucet
ops.body: Thread a short length of PVC pipe into the 1/2" pipe flange, put an elbow on top of it, and a second short piece out of the elbow to form the spout. The flange is the base — it screws flat to the cabinet top behind the basin — so make the upright tall enough that the elbow clears the basin rim, and the spout long enough that it actually overhangs the water. Dry-fit the whole assembly on the cabinet and hold it where it will live before you commit to any length. Spray paint the assembled faucet, letting it dry fully between coats, and paint the knob hardware at the same time so the whole set matches.

ops.title: Add the spinning knobs
ops.body: Drill a hole through the cabinet top on each side of the faucet, sized to the machine screw's shank so the screw turns freely in the wood rather than threading into it — that free turn is the entire trick, and a hole drilled tight kills it. Pass each screw up from underneath, run a nut on above the top, and cap the exposed end so the cap spins on the screw. Tighten the underside nut only enough to stop the screw dropping through, then test that both knobs turn easily before you move on.

ops.title: Install the pump
ops.body: Sit the submersible pump in the bottom of the basin and push one end of the vinyl tubing onto its outlet. Route the tubing out of the basin, down inside the cabinet, and back up through the PVC faucet so its free end sits just inside the spout — the 3 ft on the materials list is what makes that route possible, so lead it in gentle curves and keep it off tight bends, which will throttle the flow. Fill the basin, run the pump, and check for leaks at the spout and around the basin rim before you hand it over. Route the pump's cord out of the back and secure it where a child cannot pull on it.

baseStepTitles: Choose the basin and size the cabinet

flags: SHORTFALL (2x4): four 18-1/2" legs and four 7-1/4" leg cleats total 103" of stock, so a single 8 ft (96") 2x4 is about 7" short. The material line reads '2x4, 8 ft or longer' — verified with packBoards at a 1/8" kerf, a 10 ft board carries all eight pieces on its own.

CUT-LIST DEFECT: the drawer bottom is listed 20-1/2" x 19", but a box built from 18" sides with the 19" front and back between them finishes 20-1/2" x 18". The 20-1/2" is confirmed by the plan's own 21-1/2" opening less 1/2" slide clearance per side; the 19" is an inch long. Written into the prose as 'trim the bottom to the assembled box'; no cut-list row was changed.

Case geometry is DERIVED, not script-proved — scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box for this plan. The 23" back read as the case's outside width, less a 3/4" 1x8 side at each end, gives exactly the 21-1/2" inside opening the plan's own description states, and the 22-3/4" drawer face is 1/4" under that 23" front. Both come from the plan's own numbers.
Finished height is not stated anywhere; 18-1/2" legs plus the 1-1/2" 2x8 top comes to 20".
No finish material (stain, poly, primer, paint) appears in the materials though the last step says to sand and finish.
Lumber otherwise verified sufficient at a 1/8" kerf: 1x8 (82-1/4" of 96"), 2x8 (three 30" top boards, 90-1/4" of 96"), 1x4 (74" of 96").

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Cut everything except the drawer box now; those parts wait until you have measured the real opening. From the 2x4: four legs 18-1/2" and four leg cleats 7-1/4" — cut the cleats to the actual width of your 1x8, which is nominally 7-1/4" but varies board to board, because a cleat that does not match the side will show as a step at the corner. Those eight pieces come to 103" of 2x4, so a single 8 ft board is about 7" short; the material line allows a longer board, and one 10 ft 2x4 carries all eight with room over. From the 1x8: two sides 18-1/4", one back 23", and the drawer face 22-3/4". From the 2x8: three top boards 30", which side by side give a top 30" wide and about 21-3/4" deep. Square every cut and label the pieces as they come off the saw — the 18-1/4" sides and the 18-1/2" legs are only a quarter inch apart and are easy to confuse.

ops.title: Build the side assemblies
ops.body: Each side assembly is one 18-1/4" 1x8 side with a 7-1/4" 2x4 leg cleat at each end. Run glue on the cleat, set the 1x8 on it with the outside edges lined up, and drive 1-1/4" or 1-1/2" brad nails through the 1x8 into the cleat. Hold the parts flush at the TOP edge and push any width discrepancy to the bottom — the top edge is what shows under the tabletop and the bottom is hidden, so that is where a mismatch should land. Build two of these and stack them face to face to confirm they are identical before going on.

ops.title: Attach the back
ops.body: Set the 23" back board across the rear of the two side assemblies, flush with their top edges, and fasten it with 1-1/4" or 1-1/2" brad nails and glue into the leg cleats and the legs. That 23" is the case's outside width, so with a 3/4" 1x8 side inside it at each end you are left with the 21-1/2" inside opening the drawer slides are sized for. Measure that opening now, before any drawer part is cut — everything downstream depends on it being what the plan assumes.

ops.title: Build and fit the drawer
ops.body: Confirm your measured opening matches the plan's assumptions — 21-1/2" inside width, 3/4" material, and 1/2" of slide clearance per side, which puts the drawer box at 20-1/2" wide — and adjust the parts if it does not. Cut the 1x4 front and back 19" and the two sides 18"; with the 19" pieces between the two 3/4" sides the box finishes 20-1/2" wide by 18" front to back. Note that the cut list gives the plywood bottom as 20-1/2" x 19": the 20-1/2" is right, but the 19" is an inch long for an 18"-deep box, so cut the bottom to the box you actually built. Assemble the box square, nail and glue the bottom on with 3/4" brad nails, and keep it perfectly flush all round so nothing rubs the slides. Mount the drawer members at the same height on both sides, slide the box in, and check it runs freely before you go any further.

ops.title: Fit the drawer face
ops.body: The face is 22-3/4", a quarter inch under the 23" front opening, which leaves an even 1/8" gap at each side. With the drawer closed, hold the face in position and shim it until the reveal is even at the top and both sides, then glue and pin it with 1-1/4" brad nails driven through the face into the drawer box front. Keep the nails well clear of wherever the knob or pull will go, or you will hit one when you drill. Sand everything, easing the top edges and corners where hands land, and finish.

flags: Lumber verified sufficient at a 1/8" kerf with packBoards: 1x6 (three 8 ft boards each yielding two 30" and two 15" planks, the last 30" and 15" off the 4 ft board), 4x4 (one 54" and one 21" post per board), 2x6 (two 47-1/2" caps per board — 95-1/8" of 96", only 7/8" spare), 2x4 (four boards exactly), 2x2 (one board), 1x10 (one 75" face per board), 1x3 (two 38-1/2" slats per board, six boards).

The 1x4 panel frame reads as FACE-APPLIED, not butted above and below the planks: the footboard planks are 15" and the footboard posts 21", so a butted frame would make the panel 22" and overrun the posts. The plan's own 'measure and cut to fit' note on the frame pieces is what covers this, and the rewrite does not assert a finished footboard panel height.

'Headboard top rail' and 'Footboard top rail' are each quantity 2 — four 45-1/2" 2x4s in all — while the steps describe attaching 'the top 2x4 rail' in the singular. Presumably doubled or stacked; the plan's data does not settle it, so the rewrite says 'the top rail (two per end)' and no more.
Slat spacing is DERIVED, not authored: twelve 2-1/2" slats use 30" of run, so over the 75" the side-rail faces cover, even spacing leaves gaps of roughly 4".

scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs returned one determination for this plan pairing 'Rail cleats' with 'Headboard cleats'; those two are not a four-member rectangle in this build, and no prose here relies on that pairing either way.

The verified dimension chain that IS solid: seven 5-1/2" planks = a 38-1/2" panel, matching the 38-1/2" panel frame pieces and the 38-1/2" slats; 38-1/2" plus a 3-1/2" post at each side = the 45-1/2" top rail; the 47-1/2" cap over that overhangs 1" per end.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Cut the parts the plan fixes now, and leave everything marked measure-and-cut-to-fit until the panels and posts are dry-assembled. From the 1x6: seven headboard planks 30" and seven footboard planks 15" — each 8 ft board gives two 30" and two 15" planks (90" of 96"), and the last 30" and 15" come off the 4 ft board. From the 4x4: two headboard posts 54" and two footboard posts 21", one of each per 8 ft post. From the 2x6: four caps 47-1/2", two per board — that is 95-1/8" of a 96" board once you allow for the kerf, so start from a squared end and cut carefully. From the 2x4: two side rails 80" and two rail cleats 8-1/2", one rail plus one cleat per board, and four top rails 45-1/2", two per board. From the 2x2: two headboard cleats 30" and four footboard cleats 5-1/2". From the 1x10: one 75" side-rail face per board. From the 1x3: twelve slats 38-1/2", two per board. Label everything as it comes off the saw — the headboard and footboard parts differ only in length in several cases, and a 30" plank in the footboard stack is not obvious until the panel is glued up.

ops.title: Build the headboard panel
ops.body: Lay the seven 30" planks edge to edge, best faces up, and shuffle them until the grain and colour read well across the panel — seven 5-1/2" planks make a panel 38-1/2" wide, and that number is what every other headboard part is built around. Edge-join them: pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws from the back are the clean way if you have the jig, otherwise glue every mating edge and clamp across the panel until dry. Measure the assembled width once it is joined and confirm it really is 38-1/2". Boards run a hair narrow or wide and seven of them compound the error, and this measured width — not the number on paper — is what you cut the 1x4 frame and the top rail to.

ops.title: Frame the panel square
ops.body: Cut the two 1x4 frame pieces to the panel's measured width — 38-1/2" if it came out on the numbers — and attach them across the top and bottom of the panel with 1-1/4" brads and glue, outside edges flush with the panel's sides. Then check the panel is square by measuring both diagonals corner to corner and pushing on the long diagonal until the two match. Do this before the glue sets: a panel that is even slightly a parallelogram will fight the posts and leave a wedge-shaped gap down one side that nothing later in the build can close.

ops.title: Cap the headboard
ops.body: Measure the actual span across the tops of the two posts and cut the 45-1/2" top rail to it — the number should come out at 38-1/2" of panel plus a 3-1/2" post at each side, which is exactly 45-1/2", and if it does not, the panel or the posts have moved and it is worth finding out why now. Attach the rail with 2-1/2" screws and glue. Then set the 47-1/2" 2x6 cap on top of it: 2" longer than the rail, so it overhangs 1" at each end, with the front and back overhangs even. Screw it down from above. This cap is what gives the bed its furniture-store weight, so take the time to get the reveal the same all the way round.

ops.title: Hang the side rails and slats
ops.body: Screw the two 8-1/2" 2x4 rail cleats to the inside faces of the posts with 2-1/2" screws, both at the same height off the floor — measure from the floor on all four posts rather than from the panel, or the mattress deck will sit twisted. Build each side rail from an 80" 2x4 with a 75" 1x10 face glued and bradded to its outside, set the rails on the cleats, and lock each top corner with a 1-1/2" L bracket and a 3" straight mending plate. Then lay the twelve 38-1/2" slats across and screw them down with 2" screws: twelve 2-1/2" slats use 30" of the 75" the rail faces cover, so spacing them evenly leaves gaps of roughly 4". Work from both ends toward the middle so any accumulated error lands in one gap in the centre rather than at the foot.

baseStepTitles: Mount the panel to the posts
Build the footboard the same way
Hang the side rails and slats

flags: SHORTFALL (1x3): two 46-1/2" bottom support rails, one 45" face frame mid rail, two 47" hook panel back frame rails and six 12" mid rails total 304". Three 8 ft boards give 288". Verified with packBoards at a 1/8" kerf — four boards are needed.

SHORTFALL (1x4): the two 56" hook panel back frame sides plus the 41" hook rail top trim cannot come off two 8 ft boards, because 56" + 41" = 97" exceeds a 96" board. Each 56" side takes its own board and the 41" trim needs a third.
MARGINAL (1x12): the two 24" shaped side blanks total exactly 48", the whole length of the 4 ft board, with nothing left for the saw kerf.

UNRESOLVED: the hook panel back frame rails (47") and the hook rail top trim and cap (41") imply two different frame widths on a 48"-wide bench — 41" is what fits between two 3-1/2" 1x4 sides on a 48" unit, 47" is not. The plan's data does not settle which is right, so the rewrite tells the builder to dry-fit and trim rather than picking a side.

VERIFIED geometry (scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs): the 17-1/4" bench end panels run the full height and the 46-1/2" bench bottom sits between them, giving a 48" box — confirmed independently by the 48" face frame front rail and by three 16" back sections totalling 48". The 49-1/2" bench top therefore overhangs 3/4" per end, and 17-1/4" + 3/4" top + 56" hook frame sides = 74", exactly the back panel length.
Base moulding checks out: 48" across the front plus a 15-3/4" return at each end is 79-1/2" of the 96" length.
Cubby divider spacing inside the bench is not specified beyond the two 14" dividers, and no shelf-pin or fixed-shelf detail is given for the cubbies.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Break the sheets down first. From the 3/4" plywood strips, all 15-3/4" wide: one bench top 49-1/2", one bench bottom 46-1/2", two cubby dividers 14", and two bench end panels 17-1/4" — three of the strips cover all six pieces. From the 1x3: two bottom support rails 46-1/2", one face frame mid rail 45", two hook panel back frame rails 47", six hook panel back frame mid rails 12". From the 1x2: one face frame top rail 49-1/2", one face frame front rail 48", two face frame stiles 15-3/4". From the 1x4: two hook panel back frame sides 56" and one hook rail top trim 41". From the 1x6: the 41" top cap. From the 1x12: two 24" blanks for the shaped sides. From the 1/2" sheet: three back sections 16" x 74", cut along the length of the sheet so all three come off one panel. Three lumber checks before you buy: the 1x3 parts total 304" and three 8 ft boards give only 288", so get a fourth; a 56" frame side and the 41" trim will not share a board (97" on a 96" board), so the two 1x4s listed will not do and you need three; and the two 24" shaped blanks come to exactly 48", the whole 1x12, with no allowance for the kerf.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.title: Build the bench storage area
ops.body: The two 17-1/4" end panels run the full height of the bench box and the 46-1/2" bottom sits between them, which makes the box 48" wide overall — the same 48" as the face frame front rail and as the three 16" back sections together, so those two parts are your check that you have it the right way round. Stand the two 14" cubby dividers on the bottom to split the space, and join everything with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, holes placed where the face frame or the back will later hide them. Fit the 49-1/2" top last, overhanging 3/4" at each end. Measure both diagonals across the front and adjust until they match before the last screws go in.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.title: Add the bottom supports
ops.body: The two 1x3 rails are 46-1/2" — the same length as the bench bottom — so they run the length of the box, not across it. Fit one under the front edge of the bottom and one under the back, fastening with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws into the bottom and into the end panels. This is what puts a sitter's weight on a rail that reaches the floor instead of hanging the whole load off the cubby dividers and the pocket screws holding them.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Build the frame flat on the bench from the 49-1/2" top rail, the 48" front rail, the two 15-3/4" stiles and the 45" mid rail, joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Check it square by its diagonals while it is still flat and unattached — a frame assembled flat is easy to keep square, and one built piece by piece against the box is not. Then glue it on and set 1-1/4" finish nails through the face, punching the heads below the surface so they fill cleanly. The frame is what covers the plywood edges, and it is the single thing that makes this read as furniture rather than a plywood box.

ops.title: Add the base moulding
ops.body: Cut the 2-1/4" base moulding to wrap the bottom of the bench: 48" across the front and a 15-3/4" return along each end, with the two front corners mitered at 45°. That is 79-1/2" of the 96" length, so there is stock spare for a mis-cut. Cut the front piece first with both miters, fit it, then cut each return with one miter and one square end at the back. Nail it on with 1-1/4" finish nails into the end panels and the bottom support rails. If you want a chunkier base, the plan's own alternative is to raise the bench bottom 1" and run 3-1/4" moulding instead of the 2-1/4".

ops.title: Build the hook panel back frame
ops.body: The two 1x4 sides are 56" and they stand on top of the finished bench section — 17-1/4" of end panel plus the 3/4" top is 18", and 18" plus 56" is 74", exactly the length of the back panel sections, so the back is your check on the overall height. Join the 47" rails and the six 12" mid rails to the sides with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, working on a flat surface and checking the diagonals. One thing to sort out before you screw anything home: the 47" rails and the 41" top trim and cap imply two different frame widths on a 48" bench, and the plan's own numbers do not settle which is right — dry-fit the frame on the bench, mark the rails against the width you actually have, and trim them to that.

ops.title: Add the top moulding
ops.body: Attach the 41" 1x4 top trim across the face of the hook panel frame at the top, glue and 1-1/4" finish nails, then set the 41" 1x6 cap flat on top of it and nail down through the cap into the trim and the frame sides. Keep the cap's overhang even at the front and at both ends — this is at eye level on a piece nearly 6 ft tall, and an uneven reveal there is the first thing anyone notices.

ops.title: Shape and attach the hook panel sides
ops.body: Mark the shaped profile on one 24" 1x12 blank and cut it with the jigsaw, staying a little outside the line rather than trying to cut to it. Then clamp the two blanks together face to face and sand the pair down to the line as one piece — that is what guarantees the two sides mirror each other exactly, and on a piece this prominent a hand-shaped profile that does not mirror is immediately visible. Keep the sander square to the edge so the profile stays perpendicular through the full 3/4" thickness. Glue and fasten them in place with 1-1/4" finish nails.

ops.title: Attach the back and finish
ops.body: Nail the three 16" x 74" back sections on with 1-1/4" finish nails every 6" into the frame members behind them, keeping the outer edges flush with the sides of the unit. Three separate boards rather than one 48" panel is deliberate — it is what lets the finished piece still go through a doorway or into a car. Mark all five coat hooks before drilling any of them, spacing them evenly across the hook rail, and drive their screws into the 1x3 frame members rather than into the 1/2" back alone, which will not hold a loaded coat. Sand every surface, fill the nail holes, and prime and paint or stain and seal.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frame
Build the hook panel back frame
Shape and attach the hook panel sides

flags: SHORTFALL (1x3): four 17-1/4" end rails, two 35-1/4" front legs and one 58-1/2" front bottom rail total 198". Two 8 ft boards give 192". Verified with packBoards at a 1/8" kerf — three boards are needed.

LIKELY SHORTFALL (1/4" plywood): eight drawer bottoms (21" x 27-5/8"), eight drawer faces (28-5/8" x 5-13/16"), two end panels (19" x 28-1/4") and the 29-1/2" x 63" back total about 8,900 square inches against the 9,216 in two 4x8 sheets — under 4% waste, which no realistic cut layout achieves. Plan on a third sheet. This is a sheet-nesting estimate, not a packBoards result.
The 3/4" x 1" stock for the eight drawer-front outer frames has no cut-list row; the materials note says it is ripped from the 1x4s, but no quantity is stated.
'2x4, 8 ft' appears in the materials and in the frame step's material list but has no cut-list row at all.
The feet have no cut-list row either, though a whole step describes bevelling and fitting them — so their size and taper are not recoverable from the plan's data. The rewrite does not invent them.
Quarter round is tight: eight drawer-front inner frames plus the top trim run roughly 660 linear inches against the 672" in seven 8 ft lengths, before any miter waste.

VERIFIED geometry (scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs): the 63" long rails run full length with the 26-1/2" back end rails between them (proved by the 29-1/2" x 63" back); the 26-1/2" back end rails run full with the 19" bottom corners between them (proved by the 22" x 26-1/2" case sides); and the 21" drawer sides run full with the 26-1/8" drawer ends between them, making each box 27-5/8" wide, exactly the drawer bottom width.

The 3/4" case parts DO fit one 4x8 sheet: the two 22" x 26-1/2" sides side by side across the width (44" of 48"), then the 22"-wide bottom and the 20-1/2"-wide top beside each other in the remaining 69-1/2" of length.

step.title: Cut the case, frame and drawer parts
step.body: Break the sheets down first. From the 3/4" 4x8 sheet: two case sides 22" x 26-1/2", one case top 20-1/2" x 62-1/2", one case bottom 22" x 60" — cut the two sides side by side across the sheet's width first (44" of 48"), then the bottom and the top beside each other in the 69-1/2" of length that is left, and it all comes off one sheet. The 64-1/2" x 23-1/4" top comes off the half sheet. From the 1/4" plywood: two end panels 19" x 28-1/4", eight drawer bottoms 21" x 27-5/8", eight drawer faces 28-5/8" x 5-13/16", and the 29-1/2" x 63" back — lay these out on paper before cutting, because two sheets is barely enough area for them and a third is cheap insurance. From the 2x2: three long rails 63", two front end rails 27-1/4", two back end rails 26-1/2", two bottom corners 19", which fills all four 8 ft 2x2s. From the 1x6: sixteen drawer sides 21" and sixteen drawer ends 26-1/8", four pieces per board; rip the four top-drawer sides and four top-drawer ends down to 4-1/2" wide and leave the other twenty-four at the full 5-1/2". From the 1x4: two back legs and two front plywood legs ripped to 3" and cut 35-1/4", one center divider 26-1/2", six drawer dividers 28-7/8", plus the 3/4" x 1" strip for the eight drawer-front outer frames. From the 1x3: four end rails 17-1/4", two front legs ripped to 2-1/4" and cut 35-1/4", and one front bottom rail 58-1/2" — those come to 198", so the two 1x3s on the materials list are about six inches short and you need three. Label every piece; there are two dozen part types here and several differ by less than an inch.

ops.title: Assemble the drawer boxes and framed fronts
ops.body: Each drawer box is two 21" sides running the full depth with the 26-1/8" ends between them, which makes the box 27-5/8" wide overall — the same as the plywood bottom, so the bottom is your squaring check on every one of the eight. The top two boxes use the 4-1/2" ripped sides and ends and so are 1" shallower than the other six. Drill pocket holes 1" from the edges with the holes to the outside, assemble, then nail the plywood bottoms on flush all round; a bottom proud by even a sixteenth will foul the glides. For each drawer front, glue and clamp a 3/4" x 1" outer frame onto a 1/4" plywood back and finish-nail from behind through the plywood so no nail shows on the face. Then fit the quarter-round inner frame: cut and fit the two short end pieces first, then flex the longer sides in between them, nailing at an angle into the outer frame. Drill the knob holes from the flat back so any tear-out lands where it will not be seen.

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the end panels
Assemble the drawer boxes and framed fronts

flags: ORPHAN PARTS: the two 13" cross braces (1x2, both ends 45°) and the 16-1/2" top piece (1x4) are in the cut list but no step in the plan places them. The rewrite does not invent locations for them.

TRIM LENGTHS DO NOT CLOSE: the assembled box is 22-1/2" x 16-1/2" overall, but the box top trim is 30" and the box end trim 18". Even with outward 45° miters at both ends a 22-1/2" side needs about 25-1/2" and a 16-1/2" end about 19-1/2". The rewrite tells the builder to hold each piece against the box and mark it rather than trusting the number.

The 1" awning support dowel has no matching material line — the only dowel listed is 1-1/4" x 30 in. That 30" length does hold both dowel parts (16-1/2" + 12" = 28-1/2", verified with packBoards), but one of them would then be the wrong diameter.
No awning fabric or canvas appears in the materials, though the title and summary both describe a striped awning.
Only three of the four box sides get trim (two 30" top trim, one 18" end trim, plus the 15" serving flap trim) — consistent with the flaps occupying the fourth side, but not stated.
VERIFIED geometry (scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs): the 22-1/2" box sides run the full length and the 15" box ends sit between them, proved by the 22-1/2" x 16-1/2" plywood bottom.
Lumber verified sufficient at a 1/8" kerf: one 10 ft 1x8, two 8 ft 1x2 (only 7/8" spare), one 4 ft 1x4, one 8 ft 2x2, one 30 in dowel. The existing step 1 is accurate and was left alone.

ops.title: Build the box
ops.body: The two 22-1/2" sides run the full length and the two 15" ends fit between them, which makes the box 22-1/2" long by 16-1/2" wide overall — exactly the size of the plywood bottom, so use the bottom as your squaring gauge rather than a tape. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each 15" end piece and join with 1-1/4" pocket screws, or glue and 1-1/4" brad nails if you would rather nothing showed inside a toy. Then lay the box down on the plywood bottom, pull it square to the panel's corners, and fasten the bottom on with 3/4" brad nails or staples every few inches.

ops.title: Trim the box and add the umbrella pole
ops.body: Hold each trim piece against the assembled box and mark it there rather than trusting the cut list on this one — the box finishes 22-1/2" x 16-1/2", and the listed 30" top trim and 18" end trim are longer than that box needs even with outward 45° miters at both ends, so they are either meant to run past the box or the numbers are off. Cut the miters, then glue and 1-1/4" brad nail the trim around the top edge. Drill for the 1-1/4" dowel umbrella pole and fasten it with 1-1/4" screws driven from inside the box, checking it stands plumb with a square from two directions before the screws go tight — the awning and everything above it hangs off this one post. Set the assembly aside.

ops.title: Build the awning
ops.body: The awning is its own small frame: the two 16-3/4" awning trim pieces with the 13-1/2" awning end trim across them, joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, or glue and 1-1/4" brads. Build it flat on the bench and check its diagonals — it has to sit level across two legs later, and a racked frame will tilt the whole awning. Drill for the 12" support dowel and fasten it with screws so it spans the frame and gives the striped awning fabric something to drape over. No fabric is listed in the materials, so plan on supplying your own. Set the awning aside.

ops.title: Attach the awning to the legs
ops.body: Stand the two 40" 2x2 legs parallel on the bench and set the awning frame across their tops with the outside faces flush. Predrill, then drive 2-3/4" self-tapping wood screws down through the awning frame into the end grain of each leg, two per joint — a 2x2 splits readily when screwed near the end, which is exactly what these screws are doing. Check the frame is square to both legs before the second screw at each joint goes in; this sub-assembly is what sets whether the finished cart stands upright or leans.

ops.title: Attach the legs to the box
ops.body: Stand the leg-and-awning assembly against one long side of the box. The legs are 40" and carry the box as well as the awning above it, so hold the box at the height you want the serving surface for the child who will use it, mark both legs at that line, and clamp or have a helper hold it there. Drive 2-3/4" screws from inside the box outward into each leg — driving from the inside keeps every fastener hidden on the finished cart. Two or three per leg is plenty. Sight the cart from the front and confirm it stands square before the last screws go tight, then set the assembly aside.

ops.title: Build the wheel support system
ops.body: Build the wheel support from the three 1x4 pieces: the 15" top with a 4" side hanging down at each end, making a shallow U. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws, or 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. The two 4" sides are what the wheel bolts will pass through, so get them parallel to each other and square to the top — if they are out of square the two 12" wheels end up on different axes and the cart crabs sideways instead of rolling straight, which no amount of care at the drilling stage will fix.

ops.title: Hinge the serving flaps
ops.body: Hinge the two 16" 1x8 serving flaps to the 15" 1x2 serving flap trim, setting the hinges in from each end of the flap so their screws land in solid wood well away from the end grain. Do this last, with the box, legs and wheels all assembled, because this is the only point at which you can swing each flap through its full arc and confirm it clears the wheels and the legs before the hinge screws are finally tightened. Predrill every hinge screw hole — 3/4" pine splits easily near an edge and a split at a hinge is not repairable. Sand every edge and corner a child will touch before it goes into service.

baseStepTitles: Trim the box and add the umbrella pole
Attach the awning to the legs
Attach the legs to the box

flags: TIGHT MATERIAL (3/4" sheets): three sheets ripped into 15-3/4" x 8 ft strips give nine 96" strips, or 864" of run. The cut-list parts total about 856" before any kerf — over 99% utilisation. packBoards puts a first-fit layout at ten strips against a nine-strip lower bound, so it is not provably short, but the layout has to be planned on paper before the first cut and a fourth sheet is cheap insurance. NOT asserted as a shortfall.

CONTRADICTION (bench doors): the cut-list row is one piece of 1/4" plywood at 18" x 36", while the step says to cut doors from leftover 3/4" stock. Two hinges (pair, qty 2) and two knobs are listed, so there are two doors, not one 36"-wide panel. The rewrite tells the builder to measure the actual opening.

NAMING/DIMENSION MISMATCH: 'Bench dividers' are six pieces at 34-1/2", which is the span between the bench sides (36" less two 3/4" sides), not the height of a vertical partition (which would be about 15-3/4"). Six full-width horizontal pieces inside a bench is not explained by the plan. The rewrite calls for the centre partition the plan's own note demands and does not assert a divider count.

The bench box orientation is NOT script-proved (scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs resolved only the towers). What is solid is that the 36" top and bottom set the bench's 36" front, since the base moulding and edge moulding are both cut at 36".

VERIFIED geometry (towers): the 72" tower sides run the full height and the 16" shelves sit between them, giving a 17-1/2" tower — confirmed by the 17-1/2" tower backs and the 17-1/2" tower base moulding.
The single 7 ft base moulding covers the three fronts (17-1/2" + 17-1/2" + 36" = 71") but leaves only 13" for side returns.
The bench back panel has no cut-list row, though a step attaches one; its size is not recoverable from the plan's data.
The '1/4" x 2-1/2" hobby stock' the door step calls for is not in the materials.

step.title: Cut the plywood parts
step.body: Have the three 3/4" sheets ripped into 15-3/4" x 8 ft strips at the store — each sheet yields three strips, so you have nine 96" strips to cut everything from. Crosscut four tower sides 72", fourteen tower shelves 16", four tower cubby dividers 7-5/8", two bench top and bottom pieces 36", six bench dividers 34-1/2", and two bench sides 17-1/4". Those parts add up to roughly 856" against 864" of strip, so there is essentially no waste allowance here: lay the cuts out on paper first, put each 72" tower side on its own strip, and fill the 24" tail behind it with one 16" shelf and one 7-5/8" cubby divider. A fourth sheet is cheap insurance against one bad cut. From the 1/4" sheet cut two tower backs 17-1/2" x 72" plus a bench back sized to the bench box once it is built. Cut the 3-1/4" base moulding to 17-1/2" for each tower and 36" for the bench, and the edge moulding to 36".
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

ops.title: Build the two towers
ops.body: Each tower is a 72" box: the two 72" sides run the full height and the 16" shelves fit between them, which makes each tower 17-1/2" wide overall — the same 17-1/2" as its back panel and its base moulding, so those two parts are your check that you have it the right way round. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of every shelf, holes facing what will become the underside, and join with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Fix at least one shelf permanently near the middle of each tower: a 6 ft frameless box with nothing but adjustable shelves has no fixed rail holding its width, and one fixed shelf gives it one. Keep the sides parallel as you work — measure the gap at the top, middle and bottom and make all three read 16".
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

ops.title: Add the shoe cubbies
ops.body: Each tower gets two of the 7-5/8" cubby dividers, notched into each other or simply screwed together, to make the plus shape that splits one bay into four shoe cubbies. Build each plus as its own small assembly on the bench and then slide it into the tower rather than fastening it in place. Two reasons that order matters: you can size the assembly against the opening you actually ended up with rather than the one on paper, and it stays removable if you want to reconfigure the shelving later. If you would rather it stayed put, a couple of 1-1/4" pocket screws up through the shelf below will hold it without being visible.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

ops.title: Attach the tower backs
ops.body: Before nailing anything, square each tower up: measure both diagonals across the open back and push on the long one until the two match, because the back will lock in whatever shape it is nailed to. Then set the 17-1/2" x 72" back on and fasten it with 1-1/4" brads every 6" into both sides and into every fixed shelf. Backing a frameless tower is not cosmetic here — with no face frame, that thin back is the only thing resisting racking once the tower is loaded and somebody leans on a door or hangs off a shelf.

ops.title: Build the bench box
ops.body: The 36" top and bottom set the bench's 36" front — the same 36" as the base moulding and the edge moulding, which is your check — and the two 17-1/4" sides give it its height. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, or nails and glue, whichever you used on the towers, and check the diagonals before the last fasteners go in. Then add at least one full-depth vertical partition at the centre, cut to the inside height of the box you just built rather than to a number: an unsupported 36" span of 3/4" plywood will sag under sitting weight over time, and that partition is also what a pair of bench doors will close against.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

ops.title: Back the bench
ops.body: Square the bench box the same way — both diagonals equal — then cut a 1/4" back to the box's actual outside dimensions and nail it on with 1-1/4" brads every 6" into the top, bottom, sides and the centre partition. The plan gives no cut-list row for this piece, so take it off the finished box. It matters as much here as on the towers: this is the one part of the mudroom system that gets sat on, and a back nailed to a square box is what keeps it square under that load.

ops.title: Add base moulding
ops.body: Nail a 17-1/2" length of the 3-1/4" base moulding across the bottom of each tower and a 36" length across the bottom of the bench, using 1-1/4" brads into the box bottom and the sides. Those three fronts use 71" of the 7 ft length, so there is only about a foot left — if you want mitered returns down the outer sides, buy a second length before you start rather than discovering it after the first miter. Then glue and pin the 3/4" edge moulding along the front edge of the bench top. A solid moulding survives shoes and heels far better than iron-on edge banding, and the front edge of a mudroom bench is the single most abused surface in this whole system.

ops.title: Add doors or a center divider
ops.body: If you want doors, measure the two openings the centre partition left you and cut a door for each, sized to leave an even 1/8" gap all round — measure the real opening, because the plan's own numbers disagree here: the cut list shows one 18" x 36" piece of 1/4" plywood while this step calls for leftover 3/4" stock, and the two pairs of hinges and two knobs supplied mean two doors, not one panel. Dress each door's edges with a strip of decorative 1/4" x 2-1/2" stock, hang them on the hinges, and fit the knobs last so you can adjust the fit without them in the way. If doors are more than you want to take on, a plain centre divider glued and nailed in place is a perfectly good finish — and it is the same partition the bench top needs for support anyway, so you lose nothing structural by stopping there.

baseStepTitles: Add doors or a center divider

flags: UNSIZED PART: the description calls for a straight centre stretcher spanning between the two sawhorses, but no cut-list row is long enough to be it — the only straight 2x6 parts are the two 26" base supports, one per sawhorse. The rewrite has the builder cut it to fit from the spare 2x6: packBoards puts all the 2x6 cut-list parts in eight 8 ft boards at a 1/8" kerf and nine are supplied, so exactly one board is free for it.

'Add the middle base support' (step 5 in the original) has no matching cut-list row either, and may be the same part as the centre stretcher. Not resolved by the plan's data; the rewrite says so rather than merging the steps.
ODD COUNTS: three 39-1/2" angled stretchers are listed for two sawhorses, and five 34" cross supports for a two-sawhorse base. Neither count is explained anywhere in the plan.

OPTIONAL FRAME (conditional shortfall): the cut list shows four frame pieces at 88", which would need four 8 ft 2x4s since two 88" pieces cannot share a 96" board — only three are listed. But the row's own note says 'cut to fit around the tabletop', and mitered around the 22" x 88" top the two long sides run about 91" and the two ends about 25", which packBoards fits in three boards. Cut to fit and three is enough; cut four literal 88" pieces and it is not.
Lumber otherwise verified sufficient at a 1/8" kerf: 2x6 (eight of nine boards consumed), 2x4 (four of seven boards).
scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box for this plan, so no prose here asserts which member of any joint is captured.

Table height is not stated anywhere. The 27-3/8" legs are cut long point to short point with 10° bevels, so with the 1-1/2" base support and the 1-1/2" top the finished height lands around 30" — normal dining height, but derived, not authored.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 2x6: four tabletop boards 88" (one per 8 ft board), eight sawhorse legs 27-3/8" measured long point to short point with both ends beveled 10°, four short base supports 7-7/8" long point to long point with both ends cut 10° off square and not parallel, and two straight base supports 26". The legs and supports pack into four more 8 ft boards, so eight of the nine 2x6s on the list are consumed — keep the ninth, because a later step needs a stretcher the cut list never sizes. From the 2x4: three angled stretchers 39-1/2" long point to long point, both ends beveled 45° and not parallel, and five cross supports 34" with one end beveled 40° and the other 50". If your saw will not reach 50°, cut both ends of the cross supports at 45° instead and shift each one along its stretcher to make up the difference — the plan offers that fallback deliberately. Set the saw once per angle and cut all the matching pieces in that one setting: eight legs that are not identical will not stand level, and no amount of shimming afterwards fixes it.

ops.title: Build the sawhorse leg sets
ops.body: Each sawhorse is one 26" straight base support with a 7-7/8" short support at each end and two legs splaying off it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the top end of every leg, then fasten the legs to the supports with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue, letting the 10° bevel at the top of each leg sit flat against the underside of the support so the leg leans outward rather than standing plumb. Work on a flat surface. Build both sawhorses, then set them side by side and measure the spread between the feet on each — if the two differ, the table will rock on whichever pair is narrower no matter how flat the top is.

ops.title: Attach the angled stretchers
ops.body: Fit an angled stretcher between the two splayed legs of each sawhorse, so its 45° bevels land flat against the inside faces of the legs. Hold it in position, mark both legs, then fasten with countersunk 2-1/2" self-tapping screws or 2-1/2" pocket hole screws driven from the back face where they will not show. Set both stretchers at the same height off the floor on the two sawhorses. Note that the cut list gives three 39-1/2" stretchers for two sawhorses — use two here and set the third aside; where the third one goes is not settled by the plan's own dimensions, so check the photo before you fit it.

ops.title: Attach the straight stretcher
ops.body: This is the one part the cut list does not size. The plan calls for a straight stretcher running the length of the table between the two sawhorse assemblies, but every straight 2x6 row is already spoken for — the two 26" base supports are one per sawhorse. Stand the two sawhorses under the 88" tabletop at the spacing you want, far enough in from the ends that nobody kicks a leg and close enough together that the top does not sag between them, measure the actual gap, and cut a stretcher to that length from the spare 2x6. Centre it on both base supports and fasten with countersunk 2-1/2" self-tapping screws or pocket holes. This piece is what stops the base folding lengthwise, so do not skip it because the cut list forgot it.

ops.title: Lay out and attach the tabletop
ops.body: Lay the four 88" tabletop boards flat and edge to edge, best faces up — four 5-1/2" boards make a top 22" wide by 88" long. Shuffle them for grain and colour, push them tight, and pull the ends into line so both ends of the top are square. Now set the sawhorse base upside down on the underside, inset from each end, and drive 2-1/2" self-tapping screws up through the base supports into the boards — at least two screws into every board, so no board can cup or twist on its own. Aligning the boards first and dropping the base onto them is far easier than trying to straighten boards under a base that is already fixed, which is why the plan is explicit about this order.

ops.title: Add the middle base support
ops.body: Add the middle base support with glue and 2-1/2" screws, centred on the run between the two sawhorses. The plan gives this part no cut-list row of its own, so take its length off the assembly in front of you rather than from a number — and if you already cut a centre stretcher to fit in the previous step, check the photo before adding a second piece, because the plan's data does not make clear whether these are two parts or one described twice.

ops.title: Add the cross supports
ops.body: Fasten the 34" cross supports underneath, each one running from the stretcher up to the underside of the tabletop, with its 40° bevel landing flat at one end and its 50° bevel at the other. Predrill before driving the 2-1/2" self-tapping screws — a bevel-cut end is short grain and splits readily. If you used the 45°/45° fallback because your saw would not reach 50°, slide each support along the stretcher until both beveled faces sit flat against their mating surfaces before you fasten it; with the fallback, position is what makes up for the angle you could not cut.

ops.title: Add the optional table frame
ops.body: If you want the framed look, wrap a 2x4 frame around the outside edge of the 22" x 88" top, mitering the four corners, and fasten it with 2-1/2" screws driven from underneath so nothing shows on the finished surface. Cut to fit rather than to the cut list's 88": mitered around the top, the two long sides come out about 91" and the two ends about 25", and that does come out of the three 8 ft boards listed. Four literal 88" pieces would not — two 88" lengths will not share a 96" board, so that reading needs four boards, not three. Cut the two long pieces first, fit them, then measure the ends off the assembly.

ops.title: Finish
ops.body: Sand every surface, and take the time to ease the top edges and the corners of the tabletop and the leg bottoms — this is an outdoor table people will lean on with bare forearms. Then prime and paint, or use an exterior stain. Whichever you choose, get finish onto the end grain of all four tabletop boards and the bottoms of the eight legs before the table goes outside: end grain drinks water, and the leg bottoms sit in whatever puddles on the patio.
materials: Primer and paint, or exterior stain

baseStepTitles: Lay out and attach the tabletop

flags: DOOR PARTS DO NOT CLOSE: the centre divider splits the 36" interior into two 17-5/8" bays (the two 17-5/8" basket-bay shelves confirm the width), so two doors across the cabinet bay come out under 9" wide each. The listed door frame parts — 16-1/4" tops and 21-1/4" sides, 2-1/2" wide stock — describe a door roughly 21" square, and the 16" x 26" plywood door panels are longer than either reading allows. The rewrite tells the builder to take the opening off the finished face frame, which is also what the plan's own note says.
Only two 'Door frame tops' are listed for two doors, and no bottom rails at all.

The 'Bottom shelf' note reads 'positioned so its top is 7-3/4" from the top of the cabinet', which does not reconcile with the 35-3/4" centre divider or the door heights — the reference point looks inverted. Not guessed at in the prose.
No stain, paint, primer or poly appears in the materials, though the final step says to finish; sandpaper is the only line there.

Case geometry is DERIVED, not script-proved — scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box here. The 36" bottom shelf plus a 3/4" side at each end gives 37-1/2", exactly the width of the 1/4" back panel, and the 39-1/2" top over that overhangs 1" per end and matches the 39-1/2" face frame top rail. Both checks come from the plan's own numbers.

Lumber verified sufficient at a 1/8" kerf: the seven 3/4" case parts fit in three of the four 11-1/4" strips from one sheet; the 1x4 footer parts (61-1/2") fit one 6 ft board; the 1x2 face frame parts fit two 8 ft boards; the 1x3 door parts fit two 6 ft boards; the back and door panels fit one 1/4" sheet.

step.title: Cut the case and trim parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" sheet into 11-1/4" strips — four strips per sheet — and crosscut the case parts from three of them: two sides 39-1/4", one top 39-1/2", one bottom shelf 36", one centre divider 35-3/4", and two basket-bay shelves 17-5/8". They pack three to a strip and one sheet covers all seven. From the 1x4: one footer front 39" and two footer sides 11-1/4". From the 1x2: three face frame stiles 35-3/4" and one face frame top rail 39-1/2". From the 1/4" sheet: the 37-1/2" x 39" back panel, and hold off on the door panels until the doors are sized. Leave the 1x3 door frame parts uncut entirely for now — the plan's own note says to cut them to your actual openings, and on this build that is not boilerplate. Label the sides, top, bottom shelf and divider as they come off the saw; four of them are within four inches of each other and only the sides are a matched pair.

ops.title: Build the box
ops.body: Assemble the two 39-1/4" sides, the 39-1/2" top, the 36" bottom shelf and the 35-3/4" centre divider with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, keeping every pocket hole where the face frame, the back or a shelf will later hide it. The 36" bottom shelf plus a 3/4" side at each end makes the case 37-1/2" across — which is exactly the width of the 1/4" back panel, so hold the back up to the case as your check before the glue sets. The 39-1/2" top then overhangs 1" at each end. Measure both diagonals and push on the long one until the two match: a wide case with a centre divider is stiff in every direction except the one that matters, and it will hold whatever shape it dries in.

ops.title: Attach the back and add the basket shelves
ops.body: Cut the 37-1/2" x 39" back and nail it on with glue and 1" brads every 6" into the sides, the top, the bottom shelf and the centre divider — square the case one more time first, because this panel is what locks the box permanently into whatever shape it is nailed to. Then fit the two 17-5/8" basket-bay shelves, which is the width of the open bay beside the divider. Fasten each one to the centre divider on one end and the side on the other, using pocket screws from underneath or brads and glue, and set them at the heights your baskets actually need rather than to a number — the plan's own note about the bottom shelf's position does not reconcile with the rest of its dimensions, so measure your baskets.

ops.title: Add the footer
ops.body: Attach the two 11-1/4" footer sides first, flush with the outside faces of the case sides, then the 39" footer front across them — glue plus 1-1/4" brads into the case bottom and into the ends of the side pieces. Fitting the sides first means the front piece hides their end grain at the corners. Keep the top edge of the footer tight against the underside of the case all the way round; a gap there reads as a shadow line and undoes the built-in look the footer exists to create.

ops.title: Build and hang the doors
ops.body: Measure your two actual door openings off the finished face frame before you cut a single door part, because the cut list's door numbers do not close against this cabinet: the centre divider leaves a 17-5/8" cabinet bay, so two doors across it come out under 9" wide each, while the listed 16-1/4" rails and 21-1/4" stiles describe a door about 21" square and the 16" x 26" panels are longer than either reading allows. Take each opening off the frame, size the door to leave an even 1/8" gap all round, then cut the 1x3 frame parts and trim the 1/4" panel to suit. Assemble each frame around its panel flat on the bench, check it square, and hang it on the concealed hinges. Fit the knobs last, once the gaps are even and both doors close without binding.

ops.title: Sand and finish
ops.body: Take the doors off and pull the knobs before finishing — it is faster than cutting in around hardware and the edges get properly covered. Fill every visible nail hole and any gaps at the face frame joints, let the filler dry, then sand the whole piece through the grits, easing the front edges of the top and the shelves where hands and baskets pass. Get finish onto the underside of the top and the inside of the bays as well as the show faces, so the panels take up moisture evenly and the case stays flat. Rehang the doors and refit the knobs when everything is dry.

baseStepTitles: Attach the back and add the basket shelves
Build and attach the face frame
